title: Why we apologize for things that aren't our fault
summary: Argue why people often apologize for situations they did not cause, and explore whether this habit is useful, harmful, or something more complicated.
topic: Why we apologize for things that aren't our fault
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing why people apologize even when they are not responsible for a problem. Give at least one specific example — real or imagined. Use present perfect for observations, a second-conditional sentence, at least one concession with 'although' or 'however', and modal verbs to express possibility and advice.
hintStarters: I've noticed that the word 'sorry' sometimes functions less as an admission of guilt and more as…
I'd argue that apologizing for things you haven't done is not always a sign of weakness — it can be…
If we only apologized when we were genuinely at fault, our conversations would be more honest, but they would also be…

hintVocab.meaningVi: chất bôi trơn xã hội — thứ giúp các tương tác trở nên dễ chịu hơn
lời xin lỗi phản xạ (không có chủ ý)
thừa nhận sự khó chịu của ai đó
hạ thấp nhu cầu của chính mình
có xu hướng tránh xung đột
làm giảm giá trị của lời xin lỗi
được rèn luyện/điều kiện hóa để
ma sát xã hội — sự va chạm trong giao tiếp

sampleText:
In a café in Amsterdam, I once watched a man bump into a chair that someone had left sticking out into the aisle. He apologised — to no one in particular, to the chair, to the general situation. Nobody was listening. The chair said nothing. But he had said sorry before he had even processed what had happened, and he walked away looking slightly more at ease, as though the word had done something useful. I have observed this kind of reflexive apology in nearly every country I have spent time in, though the frequency and the setting vary. In some places, 'sorry' is the first word spoken when a stranger makes eye contact on a crowded train. In others, it comes out when you ask for directions, as if the act of needing help requires an apology in advance.

Although this habit looks like a failure of confidence, I think it is more complicated than that. A reflexive apology is often not really an admission of fault. It is a way of acknowledging that something uncomfortable has happened and that you are aware of it. When you say sorry for a late train, or for the rain, or for a noisy neighbour, you are not claiming responsibility — you are signalling empathy, reducing friction, keeping the social temperature down. If people only apologised when they were genuinely at fault, some conversations would be more accurate, but they would also feel harder and colder. The reflexive apology acts as a kind of social lubricant, and it would be naive to assume that removing it would always make things better.

However, there is a cost. When apologies lose their specificity, they can lose their meaning. A person who says sorry twenty times a day may find that when a real apology is needed — one that requires genuine remorse and a commitment to change — the word no longer carries enough weight. There is also a risk that habitual apologising can quietly reinforce a belief that you are always somehow in the wrong, or that your own comfort matters less than everyone else's. You should not have to apologise for taking up space, for having needs, or for existing inconveniently. The most useful kind of apology is a precise one: given when it is genuinely owed, and withheld when it is not. Learning the difference is, as with most things involving other people, harder than it sounds.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: không nhằm vào ai cụ thể

sampleAnnotations.phrase: before he had even processed what had happened
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: trước khi anh ấy kịp hiểu điều gì đã xảy ra

sampleAnnotations.phrase: acknowledging that something uncomfortable has happened
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: thừa nhận rằng điều gì đó khó chịu đã xảy ra
duy trì nhiệt độ xã hội thấp / giữ không khí hạ nhiệt
âm thầm củng cố một niềm tin

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a precise one: given when it is genuinely owed
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một lời xin lỗi chính xác: được đưa ra khi thực sự cần thiết

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for observations across time and place
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I have observed this kind of reflexive apology in nearly every country I have spent time in' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để diễn đạt một quan sát tích lũy từ nhiều kinh nghiệm khác nhau — không gắn với một sự kiện cụ thể mà là một kết luận rút ra từ thời gian.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I have observed this kind of reflexive apology in nearly every country I have spent time in' uses the present perfect to express an observation built from many different experiences — not tied to a single event but drawn from time.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for a thought experiment
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If people only apologised when they were genuinely at fault, some conversations would be more accurate, but they would also feel harder and colder' dùng quá khứ đơn + would để khám phá một thế giới giả định — hậu quả của một thay đổi hành vi mà thực tế không xảy ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If people only apologised when they were genuinely at fault, some conversations would be more accurate, but they would also feel harder and colder' uses past simple + would to explore a hypothetical world — the consequences of a behavioural change that does not actually happen.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tại một quán cà phê ở Amsterdam, tôi đã xem một người đàn ông va vào một chiếc ghế mà ai đó đã để thò ra ngoài lối đi. Anh ta xin lỗi — không nhằm vào ai cụ thể, với chiếc ghế, với tình huống chung. Không ai đang lắng nghe. Chiếc ghế không nói gì. Nhưng anh ấy đã nói xin lỗi trước khi kịp hiểu điều gì đã xảy ra, và anh ta bước đi với vẻ thoải mái hơn một chút, như thể từ đó đã làm được điều gì đó hữu ích. Tôi đã quan sát loại lời xin lỗi phản xạ này ở gần như mọi quốc gia tôi đã sống, dù tần suất và bối cảnh khác nhau. Ở một số nơi, 'xin lỗi' là từ đầu tiên được nói khi một người lạ giao tiếp bằng mắt trên tàu đông người. Ở những nơi khác, nó xuất hiện khi bạn hỏi đường, như thể hành động cần giúp đỡ đòi hỏi một lời xin lỗi trước.

Mặc dù thói quen này trông như sự thiếu tự tin, tôi nghĩ nó phức tạp hơn thế. Lời xin lỗi phản xạ thường không thực sự là lời thừa nhận lỗi lầm. Đó là cách thừa nhận rằng điều gì đó khó chịu đã xảy ra và bạn nhận thức được điều đó. Khi bạn nói xin lỗi vì một chuyến tàu muộn, hoặc vì mưa, hoặc vì một người hàng xóm ồn ào, bạn không nhận trách nhiệm — bạn đang báo hiệu sự đồng cảm, giảm ma sát, giữ cho nhiệt độ xã hội hạ xuống. Nếu mọi người chỉ xin lỗi khi họ thực sự có lỗi, một số cuộc trò chuyện sẽ chính xác hơn, nhưng chúng cũng sẽ cảm thấy khó khăn hơn và lạnh lùng hơn. Lời xin lỗi phản xạ hoạt động như một loại chất bôi trơn xã hội, và sẽ là ngây thơ khi cho rằng việc loại bỏ nó luôn làm mọi thứ tốt hơn.

Tuy nhiên, có một cái giá phải trả. Khi những lời xin lỗi mất đi tính cụ thể, chúng có thể mất đi ý nghĩa. Một người nói xin lỗi hai mươi lần mỗi ngày có thể thấy rằng khi cần một lời xin lỗi thực sự — một lời đòi hỏi sự ăn năn thực sự và cam kết thay đổi — từ đó không còn đủ trọng lượng nữa. Cũng có nguy cơ rằng thói quen xin lỗi có thể âm thầm củng cố một niềm tin rằng bạn luôn bằng cách nào đó sai, hoặc sự thoải mái của chính bạn ít quan trọng hơn của mọi người khác. Bạn không nên xin lỗi vì chiếm chỗ, vì có nhu cầu, hay vì tồn tại không thuận tiện. Loại lời xin lỗi hữu ích nhất là loại chính xác: được đưa ra khi thực sự cần thiết, và không đưa ra khi không cần. Học cách phân biệt sự khác biệt, như với hầu hết mọi thứ liên quan đến người khác, khó hơn nghe có vẻ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect to describe an accumulated observation?
options: I have observed this kind of reflexive apology in nearly every country I have spent time in.
I observed this kind of reflexive apology in nearly every country I have spent time in.
I have observed this kind of reflexive apology in nearly every country I spent time in.
I am observing this kind of reflexive apology in nearly every country I have spent time in.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both clauses use the present perfect to describe experience accumulated over an unspecified period up to now.
mcQuestions.hint: Use present perfect in both clauses when the observation and the experience both extend to the present.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly to explore a hypothetical?
options: If people only apologised when they were genuinely at fault, some conversations would feel harder.
If people only apologize when they are genuinely at fault, some conversations would feel harder.
If people only apologised when they were genuinely at fault, some conversations will feel harder.
If people would only apologise when genuinely at fault, some conversations would feel harder.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + bare infinitive' in the main clause. Never 'would' in the if-clause.
mcQuestions.hint: Use past simple after 'if' and 'would + verb' in the result — never 'would' in the if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to introduce a concession?
options: Although this habit looks like a failure of confidence, I think it is more complicated than that.
Although this habit looks like a failure of confidence, but I think it is more complicated than that.
Although, this habit looks like a failure of confidence, I think it is more complicated than that.
This habit looks like a failure of confidence, although, I think it is more complicated.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but' after the comma, and no comma directly after 'although'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' is followed by the concession clause, then a comma, then the main clause — no 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which modal verb correctly gives advice in the argument?
options: You should not have to apologise for taking up space.
You must not apologise for taking up space.
You cannot apologise for taking up space.
You will not apologise for taking up space.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Should not have to' expresses a moral or social recommendation — it means this situation is not right or fair. 'Must not' is a prohibition; 'cannot' is impossibility; 'will not' is a statement of future fact.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Should not have to' signals an opinion about what is fair or right.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly means an automatic, unthinking apology?
options: reflexive apology
automatic sorry
habitual excuse
immediate regret
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Reflexive apology' is the standard term for an apology made automatically, before thinking.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Reflexive' describes an action done without conscious thought, like a reflex.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'however' at the start to introduce a contrast?
options: However, there is a cost.
However there is a cost.
There is, however a cost.
There is however, a cost.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' opens a sentence as a contrast discourse marker, it must be followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: Sentence-initial 'however' always needs a comma directly after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly without repeating the subject?
options: When a real apology is needed — one that requires genuine remorse and a commitment to change — the word no longer carries enough weight.
When a real apology is needed — one that it requires genuine remorse and a commitment to change — the word no longer carries enough weight.
When a real apology is needed — one which requires it genuine remorse and a commitment to change — the word no longer carries weight.
When a real apology is needed — one requires genuine remorse and a commitment to change — the word no longer carries weight.
mcQuestions.explanation: In the relative clause 'one that requires…', 'that' is already the subject — do not add 'it'.
mcQuestions.hint: After a relative pronoun like 'that', never add a pronoun to repeat the subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the phrase 'come across as'?
options: An over-apologetic person can come across as lacking confidence.
An over-apologetic person can come across like lacking confidence.
An over-apologetic person can come across to lack confidence.
An over-apologetic person can come across as to lack confidence.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Come across as + adjective/noun' is the correct structure. Do not use 'like', 'to', or 'as to' after 'come across as'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Come across as' is followed directly by an adjective or noun phrase.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure correctly in a list after 'apologise for'?
options: You should not have to apologise for taking up space, for having needs, or for existing inconveniently.
You should not have to apologise for taking up space, for having needs, or to exist inconveniently.
You should not have to apologise for taking up space, having needs, or existing inconveniently by you.
You should not have to apologise for taking up space, for needs, or for existing inconveniently.
mcQuestions.explanation: Each item after 'apologise for' should use 'for + gerund': 'for taking', 'for having', 'for existing'.
mcQuestions.hint: After a preposition like 'for', use the gerund (-ing form) consistently across all parallel items.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase correctly means to reduce social conflict or awkwardness?
options: smooth things over
flat things out
ease things away
calm things off
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Smooth things over' is the fixed phrasal expression for resolving tension or awkwardness in a social situation.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a fixed phrasal verb — only 'smooth' and 'over' combine in this meaning.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'no longer' in the correct position?
options: The word no longer carries enough weight.
The word carries no longer enough weight.
No longer the word carries enough weight.
The word carries enough no longer weight.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'No longer' precedes the main verb (or the auxiliary if there is one): 'no longer carries'.
mcQuestions.hint: Place 'no longer' directly before the main verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If apologising for things outside your control is largely performative, does that make it dishonest? Or is there a kind of social honesty in using language to manage relationships rather than to report facts?
